# 📚 Lessons & Playbook — Notion brain snapshot

> Durable backup/mirror of the Lessons & Playbook rulebook ([source](https://www.notion.so/38f829ebf7be81c5a62ccb3bdb63c603)).
> Canonical working brain is [`CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md). Regenerate via the
> **Snapshot Notion brain** GitHub Action (scheduled + manual).

> ⭐ **The brain's standing rulebook.** Every run reads this page FIRST, before acting. Every failure or lesson learned gets appended here. These rules are hard-won — they exist because the mistake already happened once. Do not relearn them.

> 

> 🤝 **Before starting ANY new build:** read [🤝 Coordination Protocol — Cowork ↔ Claude Code](https://app.notion.com/p/390829ebf7be81e09866e1cd3a6bed6e) and check the [🔗 Work Claims Ledger](https://app.notion.com/p/63dec393c18546ee9b924a472e9c59a3) for the target repo. Create a Claimed row before writing code. This is what prevents two sessions building the same feature two incompatible ways (it already happened once — see lesson #16 below and the ledger's own history).

## 🧭 The Lessons (rulebook)

Each row: the mistake/lesson → the rule that prevents it → where it applies.


| # | Date added | Lesson / mistake | The rule that prevents it | Where it applies |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 2026-07-01 | Git auth using `username:<TOKEN>@github.com` triggered a false "token is read-only" alarm — the token actually works fine for push. | Always authenticate as `x-access-token:<TOKEN>@github.com`. NEVER use `username:<TOKEN>`. If a push looks rejected, re-check the auth form before believing the token is broken. | Any git clone/push from the sandbox; all 5 repos incl. private. |
| 2 | 2026-07-01 | Repeatedly "retrying" PR opens against `api.github.com` that can never succeed from the cloud sandbox. | `api.github.com` (REST) is BLOCKED from the cloud sandbox. You can push branches but cannot open or merge PRs via REST. The merge happens via the GitHub Actions auto-merge workflow (runs on GitHub's servers) or via Claude Code locally. Push the branch, then stop — do not retry PR opens. | Cloud-sandbox sessions doing GitHub work. (Claude Code local has a working GitHub MCP and is exempt.) |
| 3 | 2026-07-01 | Sandbox ran out of disk mid-clone after accumulating old clones/caches; retried the clone repeatedly and kept failing. | Preflight `df -h` before cloning. Clean old clones/caches first. Clone shallow (`--depth 1`). If you hit "No space left on device", STOP and escalate — do NOT retry. A fresh session = a fresh disk. | Any sandbox session that clones repos. |
| 4 | 2026-07-01 | Claimed a change was "done" / a push or merge had "happened" when it had not — work reported complete without proof. | A change is "done" ONLY when the actual symptom is proven gone: reproduce → fix → prove. Label the verification level (e.g. "verified: CI green" vs "unverified: pushed only"). NEVER claim a push/merge that didn't actually happen. | Every fix, every status update, every brain note. |
| 5 | 2026-07-01 | Built work on top of an unmerged branch, deepening a stack that then all blocked on the bottom branch. | Don't stack on unmerged branches. Merge bottom-first — merging the base unblocks more work than stacking deeper does. | Multi-branch / dependent-PR work on any repo. |
| 6 | 2026-07-01 | Ground on the same failing action over and over (same error each time), wasting the session. | Loop-breaker: if an action fails ~2× the same way, CHANGE APPROACH or stop and escalate. Never grind the same failing path. | Any repeated tool/command failure. |
| 7 | 2026-07-01 | Manufactured filler work to look busy when genuinely-unblocked work had run out. | Don't manufacture filler. Validate viability before building. When the unblocked work is genuinely done, STOP and escalate rather than padding. | End-of-queue / no-clear-next-step situations. |
| 8 | 2026-07-01 | Used a weak/cheap model to plan a complex multi-step task, producing a poor plan. | Planning for complex tasks uses the TOP model (Opus / Fable), never a weak one. Execution can downshift; planning cannot. | Any complex, multi-step task kickoff. |
| 9 | 2026-07-01 | Treated files written to the outputs folder (patches, reports) as durable — they vanished next session. | Outputs-folder files are EPHEMERAL. Persist anything important to Notion, or push it to a branch. Never leave the only copy of important work in outputs. | Any session producing patches, reports, or artifacts. |
| 10 | 2026-07-01 | Acted on stale brain notes about branch/PR state that no longer matched live git. | Stale brain notes drift fast. Reconcile branch/PR claims against LIVE git before acting on them. Archive superseded notes — don't delete (keeps the audit trail). | Start of any session that reads brain status. |
| 11 | 2026-07-01 | Some watchdog sessions launch with NO git credentials at all — not even a read-only token (no `GITHUB_TOKEN`/`GH_TOKEN` env, no credential helper, no `.git-credentials`). Lesson #1 assumes a token exists; that isn't always true. Discovering it only at `git push` time wastes the build. | PRE-FLIGHT the credential before planning any push-dependent work: check `env \| grep -i token`, `git config credential.helper`, and `~/.git-credentials`. If ALL are empty, do NOT bank on pushing — build the change, verify it, then persist the full patch to a durable Notion page (lesson #9) for Aden/Claude Code to `git am`  • push. Attempt push at most once to confirm, then stop (loop-breaker). | Any cloud-sandbox session whose plan depends on pushing a branch. |
| 12 | 2026-07-01 | Concluded "zero git creds, cannot push" from empty env/helper/.git-credentials — but a PRIOR run in the same environment had left an authenticated clone on disk (/tmp/work/<repo> with an embedded x-access-token in its remote URL), so pushing was actually possible. | Before concluding you can't push: check for persisted clones from earlier runs (`find / -maxdepth 4 -name '*.git'`) and inspect their `git remote get-url origin` for an embedded `x-access-token:...@github.com`. If present, reuse that authenticated URL (fresh shallow clone into a dir you own — the root-owned clone rejects writes with `safe.directory`/permission errors). Then push once to confirm. | Any cloud-sandbox watchdog run that thinks it has no push credentials. |
| 13 | 2026-06-30 | Merging a fix into `main` doesn't retrigger already-evaluated CI checks on sibling open PRs. `rerun_failed_jobs` re-runs against the SAME stale merge-ref snapshot, not a fresh `synchronize` event — so a PR can show green/pending against pre-fix code even after the fix landed. | After fixing something on `main` that sibling PRs need, explicitly `git merge origin/main` into each open branch and push — don't rely on "rerun failed jobs" to pick up the fix. | Any repo with multiple stacked/sibling PRs open when a shared CI or source fix lands on main. |
| 14 | 2026-06-30 | Naive block-by-block git conflict resolution can leave zero conflict markers but still produce syntactically broken code — a kept opening brace/tag paired with a closing one from the discarded side (hit twice: Sadie's ImageGenerator.tsx JSX, bussnessbuilder's server.ts for-loop/.forEach mismatch). | "No markers remain" is NOT proof a conflict resolution is correct. Always rebuild/typecheck/lint after resolving conflicts. For a badly-tangled hunk, prefer replacing the whole file from a known-good commit over hand-splicing. | Any merge/rebase conflict resolution, especially JSX/brace-heavy files. |
| 15 | 2026-06-30 | A repo's `main` branch had literal unresolved `<<<<<<<`/`=======`/`>>>>>>>` markers committed and sitting there undetected — CI didn't catch it because the affected files/paths weren't exercised by the test suite. | Don't assume main is clean just because CI is green. When starting work in an unfamiliar repo, grep for conflict markers (`<<<<<<<`) across the tree once before building on top of it. | Start of any new work session in a repo you haven't touched recently. |
| 16 | 2026-06-30 | A feature-gate scaffold (Sadie's automationCenter.ts) mapped Pro-gated capabilities to aspirational/placeholder IPC channel names (`homebot:automation:scheduler:add`) that didn't match the literal channel strings actually registered in ipc-handlers.ts (`homebot:scheduler-add`). The gate code was fully correct and fully unreachable — nothing was actually gated in the running app. | When wiring a gate/guard/permission-check module against real handlers, verify the channel/route/key strings by grep against the actual registration site, not against the spec doc or an earlier scaffold's assumption. Add a real integration point (real ipcMain.handle wrapped), not just a table that looks right. | Any feature-gating, permission, or capability-check layer built ahead of its real integration. |
| 17 | 2026-06-30 | Ran a `runInBand` full Jest suite in the background piped through `tail`; output buffers until process exit, so a hung/slow suite showed zero output for ~55 minutes with no way to tell progress from a hang. | For long test runs, redirect to a log file (not a pipe) so output streams incrementally and can be tailed live, and prefer parallel (default) Jest over `--runInBand` unless test isolation truly requires it — it's far faster and fails fast instead of hanging on one slow suite. | Any long-running background test/build command. |
| 18 | 2026-06-30 | Adding a new UI button reused an existing CSS class (`.button-save`) purely for its (nonexistent) styling, not realizing 3 unrelated tests used `container.querySelector('.button-save')` as a structural selector for a DIFFERENT button — the new element became the first match and silently broke those tests. | Before reusing a generic/semantic class name in markup, grep the test suite for `querySelector`/`getBy*` usages of that class — test selectors often depend on a class being unique even when the class carries no CSS. | Any renderer/component change reusing an existing CSS class name. |
| 22 | 2026-07-01 | The in-sandbox `npm install` frequently finishes only partially — package dirs exist under `node_modules` but the toolchain is unusable: `.bin` symlinks aren't linked, `typescript/lib/tsc.js` is missing, esbuild's JS entry and vitest's run entry aren't there — so `ng lint`/`ng test`/`ng build` cannot run. Grinding the install wastes the session (confirmed again this run; matches prior "vitest could not execute" notes). | Don't bank on a full in-sandbox build/test. Attempt `npm install` at most ~1–2×, then STOP (loop-breaker). Verify what you can WITHOUT the toolchain: execute pure logic by running the real module via `node --experimental-strip-types` (Node 22 strips types natively) with inline assertions, and syntax-check every changed TS file with `node --check --experimental-strip-types`. Label the level honestly and rely on the push-triggered CI gate (lint+test+build) — the workforce-merge pipeline won't land a red branch, so main stays safe. | Any cloud-sandbox session that needs to verify TS/Angular changes it can't fully build in-sandbox. |
| 19 | 2026-07-01 | Recovering the embedded token from a prior run's on-disk clone (lesson #12): `git remote get-url origin` and `git remote -v` on that clone returned EMPTY / a `fatal: detected dubious ownership` error (the clone is owned by `nobody`, not the sandbox user), which nearly masked the fact that a working `x-access-token` WAS present. Concluding "no token" from the empty output would have been wrong. | Don't use `git remote get-url` to inspect a persisted foreign-owned clone — it errors out on `safe.directory` and prints nothing. Read the token straight from the file: `grep 'url = ' <clone>/.git/config` (or add the [safe.directory](http://safe.directory/) exception first). Then reuse that URL for a fresh clone into a dir you own and confirm with `git ls-remote`. Verified working this run: pushed `feat/toolregistry-validation-errors` to Sadie via exactly this path. | Any cloud-sandbox watchdog run recovering push creds from an earlier run's on-disk clone. |
| 20 | 2026-07-01 | Two independent sessions each built their own "cosmetics" system in Vacuum-Stimulator- (different data shapes, different Firestore paths, an add/add conflict on the same filename) because neither checked for existing/in-flight work on the same feature before building. | Before starting a new build, check the 🔗 Work Claims Ledger's Active view for the target repo AND grep the codebase for a same-purpose module. Create a Claimed row before writing code. See 🤝 Coordination Protocol. | Any new feature build, especially on a repo with multiple parallel automated sessions. |
| 21 | 2026-07-01 | Claims of "verified" / "logic-verified" work were written into brain notes for pure modules (progression.ts, rooms.ts) that had zero committed test files — the verification ran once, ephemerally, and could not be checked by the next session. | Never write "verified" in a Work Claims Ledger row or brain note without a real, committed test-file path attached. A test run that isn't persisted didn't happen as far as the next session is concerned. | Any claim of verification, in the ledger, a project page, or an EOD note. |
| 23 | 2026-07-01 | Standing rules lived only in Notion, so a Claude Code session without a Notion connector never saw them. The playbook couldn't shape local runs. | Bake the core rules into each repo's root `CLAUDE.md` under `## Operating Rules (read first)` — Claude Code auto-loads [CLAUDE.md](http://claude.md/) as context every run, so the rules propagate even with no Notion access. When creating the section, CREATE the file if missing or PREPEND above existing content (never delete existing [CLAUDE.md](http://claude.md/) content). Ship it on a `claude/**` branch and confirm the push with `git ls-remote`. | All 5 repos; any repo Claude Code works in. |
| 24 | 2026-07-01 | shootemup's `auto-merge.yml` opens PRs and enables auto-merge using the built-in `github.token` (GITHUB_TOKEN). GitHub Actions never triggers other workflows (like `android-ci.yml`'s `pull_request` trigger) from an event caused by GITHUB_TOKEN — an anti-recursion guardrail. Result: every bot-created PR (#6 PIERCE weapon, #7 Operating Rules, #8 high-score persistence) merged straight to `main` with ZERO CI runs — no build, no test — even though the workflow's whole purpose is "auto-merge once CI is green." Confirmed via `pull_request_read get_check_runs`: only the `open-pr-and-enable-auto-merge` job ever ran, no `Android CI` job. | Before trusting "CI is green" on any repo with a bot-driven open-PR-and-auto-merge workflow: check `get_check_runs` on the actual PR, not just workflow-run history for the repo. To fix: `auto-merge.yml` needs a PAT/App token (not `github.token`) when calling `gh pr create`, OR add an explicit `gh workflow run` dispatch for the CI workflow after PR creation, OR require the CI check via branch protection so at minimum merge is blocked pending a status that never arrives (fail-closed instead of fail-open). shootemup: `.github/workflows/auto-merge.yml` needs this fix — flagged 2026-07-01, not yet applied (needs Aden's call on which fix + repo settings access). | Any repo with a bot-driven open-PR / auto-merge workflow running on GITHUB_TOKEN. |

> 🩹 **Repair note 2026-07-02:** lesson #24 above was previously stored as escaped literal `<tr>/<td>` text below this table (a broken Notion edit) — it has been re-entered as a real row and the garbage removed. Table row order runs … 18, 22, 19, 20, 21, 23, 24 (row 22 was appended out of sequence); numbers are stable IDs referenced by other lessons — do not renumber.


## 🔁 How this stays live

This page is only useful if the lessons get **used**, not just stored. The loop:

1. **Read first.** Every run reads 📚 Lessons & Playbook before acting. The hub and the Manual Checklist both carry a note pointing here.
1. **Append on failure.** Every time a mistake is made or a lesson is learned, add a row to the table above (date added, lesson, rule, where it applies) before the session ends. A lesson that isn't written down will be relearned.
1. **Enforce + propagate weekly.** The weekly Brain Health task enforces these rules and propagates them into the scheduled-task run-prompts, so the rulebook actually shapes behavior rather than sitting idle. (The run-prompts are being wired to read this page directly.)
If you ever catch yourself about to do something this page warns against — stop. The cost of these lessons was already paid.


## 📌 Append template

Copy this row format when adding a lesson:

`| N | YYYY-MM-DD | what went wrong | the rule that prevents it | where it applies |`


## Lesson 25 — Re-triggering an already-pushed backlog into a live auto-merge pipeline

**2026-07-01.** Lesson / mistake: when re-triggering already-pushed branches so a newly-live `claude/**`-gated auto-merge workflow catches them, a naive `git push` of a `feat/*`/`fix/*`/`copilot/*` branch does nothing — the workflow's push trigger only fires on the `claude/**` glob, so those branches sit forever.

The rule that prevents it: (a) `claude/**` branches → re-fire with ONE empty commit (`git commit --allow-empty` then push). (b) Any non-`claude/**` branch → **mirror** it to a new `claude/<original-name>` branch (`git push origin origin/<name>:refs/heads/claude/<name>`) and leave the original alone; the `**` glob matches nested slashes so `claude/feat/foo` still triggers. (c) Confirm EVERY push with `git ls-remote` (lesson #2/#4 — merge itself is NOT confirmable from the sandbox; `api.github.com` is blocked, so watch the Actions tab). (d) SAFETY FIRST: before re-triggering a repo, confirm `.github/workflows/auto-merge.yml` actually exists on `main` (`git cat-file -e origin/main:.github/workflows/auto-merge.yml`) — if it's absent, the ci-cd-workflows PR hasn't merged yet; don't re-trigger there. (e) `git cherry origin/main <branch>` surfaces WIP/known-conflicting branches too (e.g. `followup/stash-wip`, `feat/cosmetic-skins`/lesson #20) — re-trigger relies on the CI gate to block bad merges; flag those for Aden rather than assume they should land.

Where it applies: any cloud-sandbox run re-triggering backlog branches into a `claude/**`-gated auto-merge pipeline.


## Lesson 26 — A durable "patch" from a prior run can be built on a STALE base; rebase before pushing

**2026-07-01.** Lesson / mistake: recovering a prior run's committed-but-unpushed feature branch (BusinessFlow `claude/whats-next-custom-domain`, commit `1ac5885`) and pushing it **raw** would have shipped a broken PR. `git diff main..<branch>` showed 7 files (not the 3 the feature touched): it also *deleted* `server.ts`/`server-meta.ts` and reverted `CLAUDE.md`. Root cause: the branch's base was 2 commits behind current `main` (missing #19 SEO server-meta + #20 [CLAUDE.md](http://claude.md/) Operating Rules), so a raw push carries those as spurious deletions — and here the deletion was the exact SSR static-pivot Aden had **explicitly rejected** (PR #18). Blindly re-pushing a saved branch would have re-opened a settled architectural decision.

The rule that prevents it: before pushing ANY recovered/old branch, (a) `git diff --stat main..<branch>` and confirm the file list matches ONLY the intended change — extra deletions/reverts mean a stale base; (b) find the real delta with `git merge-base main <branch>` + `git log <merge-base>..main` to see what main gained since; (c) don't push raw — **cherry-pick the feature commit onto CURRENT main** (`git checkout -b <branch> main && git cherry-pick <sha>`), then re-confirm `git diff --stat main` is exactly the intended files and spot-check that files main added since (e.g. `server.ts`, [CLAUDE.md](http://claude.md/) rules) are still present; (d) re-run verification on the rebased tree (here: 19/19 node assertions + `node --check` on all 3 changed TS) before pushing; (e) confirm with `git ls-remote`. Also: the "durable patch" Notion page for this branch only had `domain-verification.ts` verbatim — the component wiring + full diff were truncated — so the on-disk branch (`/tmp/bf`) was the only complete copy. Corollary to lesson #9: a patch page is a fallback, not a substitute for the actual commit; prefer recovering the real commit from a persisted clone.

Where it applies: any cloud-sandbox run that recovers a committed-but-unpushed branch from an earlier run (on-disk clone or Notion patch) and intends to push it.


## Lesson 27 — Partial in-sandbox npm install pulls esbuild's binary for the WRONG platform; transpile changed files directly as a Linux syntax check

**2026-07-01.** Lesson / mistake: on comand (Vite + React Three Fiber), a fresh in-sandbox `npm install` finished partially (lesson #22) in a new way — it left `node_modules/.bin` empty AND installed esbuild's optional native binary for the wrong platform (`@esbuild/aix-ppc64` present, but Linux needs `@esbuild/linux-x64`), so `vite build` died at config load with "You installed esbuild for another platform than the one you're currently using." Grinding the install would have burned the session.

The rule that prevents it: (a) don't expect a clean `vite build` in-sandbox — attempt install ~1–2× then STOP (lesson #22 loop-breaker). (b) If vite fails on the esbuild platform error, ONE targeted fix: read esbuild's version (`node -e "require('./node_modules/esbuild/package.json').version"`) and `npm install --no-save --no-audit --no-fund @esbuild/linux-x64@<that-exact-version>`; the binary lands at `node_modules/@esbuild/linux-x64/bin/esbuild`. (c) Even after that, a full `vite build` can still die on an UNRELATED third-party package whose entry didn't fully install (here: `hls.js` "Failed to resolve entry for package") — that's an environment artifact, NOT your code; stop, don't chase it. (d) Verify what you CAN without the full build: `node node_modules/typescript/lib/tsc.js --noEmit` (the `.bin/tsc` symlink is missing but the entry JS is there) and FILTER the output to your changed files — a partial install pollutes untouched files with bogus "has no exported member" errors (e.g. firebase), so judge only errors that name YOUR files; and transpile each changed `.ts`/`.tsx` directly with `node_modules/@esbuild/linux-x64/bin/esbuild <file> --jsx=automatic --format=esm >/dev/null` (NO `--loader` flag when passing a real file — esbuild infers the loader from the extension and errors "loader without extension only applies when reading from stdin" otherwise). Empty stderr = clean syntax/JSX. (e) Label the level honestly ("tsc clean on changed files + esbuild transpile clean; full vite build gated on PR CI") and rely on the `claude/**` auto-merge CI gate for the real build.

Where it applies: any cloud-sandbox run verifying a Vite/esbuild TS/TSX change it can't fully build in-sandbox (comand, and any Vite repo).


## Lesson 28 — A prior run's on-disk clone only yields a reusable token if that run was authenticated; public-anon clones have a plain remote URL

**2026-07-02.** Lesson / mistake: lessons #12/#19 say "recover the embedded `x-access-token` from an earlier run's on-disk clone." This run found three on-disk clones (`/tmp/bussnessbuilder`, `/tmp/comand`, `/tmp/Sadie`) but ALL had a plain `url = https://github.com/kingithegreat/<repo>` in `.git/config` — **no embedded token** — because they were cloned anonymously (public repos need no auth to read). So there was nothing to recover, and the two private repos (Vacuum, shootemup) couldn't be cloned at all. Time can be wasted hunting for a token that a public-anon clone never carried.

The rule that prevents it: preflight the credential decisively before planning any push. (a) `env | grep -i token`, `git config --global credential.helper`, `ls ~/.git-credentials`, and `grep 'x-access-token' /tmp/*/.git/config` — a token is recoverable ONLY if a PRIOR AUTHENTICATED run embedded it. (b) A one-shot proof: `GIT_TERMINAL_PROMPT=0 git ls-remote <a PRIVATE repo>` — if it returns `could not read Username`, you have NO push capability this session (public-repo read works anonymously and tells you nothing about push auth, so probe a private repo). (c) If all empty: do NOT bank on pushing — build the change, verify it, persist a full `git format-patch`/am-able patch to a durable Notion page (lesson #9/#11), attempt exactly ONE confirming `git push` (expect exit 128 `could not read Username`), then STOP (loop-breaker). (d) A public repo can still be cloned read-only to build + verify against real code even with no token — so an unblocked "build + patch" advance is still possible; only the push itself is gated.

Where it applies: any cloud-sandbox watchdog run that needs to push, whose only on-disk clones are public-repo anonymous clones.


## Lesson 29 — A workflow declared "replaced" must actually be DELETED, and "branch deleted" claims must be re-verified with `git ls-remote`

**2026-07-02.** Lesson / mistake: two brain-vs-reality drifts found in the same audit of bussnessbuilder. (1) `CLAUDE.md` said the old PR-based `auto-merge.yml` was "replaced" by `workforce-merge.yml` — but the file was never deleted, so BOTH workflows fired on every `claude/**` push and some branches landed twice via two different paths (PR squash + `__integrate` merge). "Replaced" in prose does nothing; only deleting the file does. (2) A 2026-06-27 brain note claimed the dangerous pre-rewrite scaffold branch `claude/lucid-gauss-mj0y7a` was "deleted" — it never was; it sat on origin for days as a standing revert hazard (merging it would have deleted `src/server.ts`) and conflicted on every hourly sweep, invisibly, because the pipeline skips conflicts with only a `::warning::` on an otherwise-green run.

The rule that prevents it: (a) when a workflow/script is superseded, the same commit that lands the replacement must `git rm` the old file — then confirm the old one is gone from `origin/main`. (b) Never write "branch/PR deleted" in the brain without proving it: `git ls-remote --heads origin <branch>` (empty output = actually gone). Same standard as lesson #4 for pushes. (c) A green pipeline run is NOT proof of a drained queue — permanently-conflicting branches are skipped silently; if a `claude/**` branch survives more than a couple of hourly sweeps, read the workforce-merge logs for its conflict and rebase-or-delete it. (d) If the sandbox's git proxy 403s on `git push --delete` (ref deletions blocked; normal pushes fine), don't grind it: force-push the branch to `origin/main`'s tip (`git push origin +origin/main:refs/heads/<branch>`) — the pipeline then classifies it as already-merged and deletes it itself on the next sweep.

Where it applies: any repo with an auto-merge pipeline; any brain note claiming a deletion/merge happened.
